body: become adults in an assistance unit in which they are the grantee or spouse of the grantee.[69] Example: Client and two children are disqualified. One of the children turns 18 and is no longer in school. The former "child" in the FA assistance unit applies for FA for her newborn. If otherwise eligible, she may receive benefits.[70] 5. Parents or step-parents who lose program eligibility pursuant to Section2960­ B cannot receive assistance for otherwise eligible children nor can such parent/step-parent be included in any assistance unit. 6. Caretaker relatives other than a parent or a stepparent must be excluded as they are not eligible. 7. Individuals removed from the assistance unit due to a permanent disqualification for failure to comply with benefit access and spending restrictions pursuant to Section 1400 cannot be included in any assistance unit. Further these disqualified grantee relatives cannot receive benefits on behalf of otherwise eligible children except for a temporary period while appointment of an alternate grantee relative is being processed.[71] 8. Individuals removed from the assistance unit for 12 months or due to a permanent disqualification pursuant to Section 2550 cannot be included in any assistance unit. Further, these disqualified grantee relatives cannot receive benefits on behalf of otherwise eligible children except for a temporary period while appointment of an alternate grantee relative is being processed. 3110 Number of Assistance Units In general, the number of assistance units is the same as the number of persons providing guidance and control for distinct sibling groups. However, certain types of extended family or multigenerational family or blended family (that is, a family composed of spouses where one or both have children living in the household from previous relationships) situations may create difficulty in terms of determining the number of assistance units in the household. In making this determination, focus on the dependent child(ren) for whom assistance is sought. A. Multiple Assistance Units Living in the Same Household Two or more assistance units exist when two or more distinct sibling groups of children with different caretakers live in the same household. These situations include the following: 11
citation_label: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2 18
heading: Page 18

metadata.title: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2

body: 1. Two sisters each with her own children live in one household and each exercises parental guidance and control over her own children; or 2. An adult daughter and her mother living in the same household and each having her own children and exercising parental guidance and control over her own children. B. Consolidated Assistance Units When an individual is required to be in two or more assistance units living in the same household, these units must be consolidated. As in the situations below, there may be two or more persons providing guidance and control for different sibling groups. The case will be set up in the name of one caretaker, the other caretaker will be shown as an additional client(s). 1. A married couple each having children by a previous marriage lives in one household. 2. An assistance unit consists of a mother and her children. If one of these children has a baby, the grandchild must be included in the senior parent's unit, if assistance is sought for the grandchild, as assistance unit provisions require that the minor parent be included in the assistance units of both her child and her siblings. C. An Assistance Unit(s) Within a Larger Household In some situations, an assistance unit(s) may exist within a larger household where assistance is not sought for all household members. This includes the following situations: 1. A child lives in the home of an aunt and her children and assistance is sought solely for the niece or nephew as the dependent child; or 2. A minor parent and her child(ren) live in the home of her parent(s) and other siblings, but assistance is sought solely for the child(ren) of the minor parent. The assistance unit would consist of the minor parent and her child(ren). 3115 Computation of the Grant Amount Although the actual grant amount will be computed by FACETS, there are some occasions when it will be necessary to determine the amount of the grant separately from the grant authorization. This may be accomplished through use of the automated payment trial budget screen (see FACETS Certification User Manual Section 8000) or by preparation of a manual budget. The steps for calculation of the grant are described below in narrative style: 12
citation_label: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2 19
heading: Page 19

metadata.title: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2

body: A. Consider all earned income for the applicant/recipient and others to be included in the budget according to policies set out in Sections 3200 - 3230. For applicants, work a trial budget as described in Section 3125-A. B. Deduct work expenses from gross earnings of each wage earner in the following order: 1. 20% of gross earnings (round all fractions of a cent to nearest cent). 2. Child care or care of an incapacitated adult (parent/stepparent) living in the same home receiving FA. C. Consider all unearned income for the applicant/recipient and others to be included in the budget according to policies set out in Sections 3200 - 3230. D. Subtract the total countable earned and unearned income rounded to the nearest dollar from the payment standard. The difference represents the entitlement amount except when overpayments are being recouped or sanction amounts are being deducted. When overpayments are being recouped, the recoupment amount is deducted from the entitlement amount before the benefits are deposited in the EBT account. When a sanction amount is being deducted, it is subtracted from the entitlement amount and the difference is the net entitlement. If the net countable income is equal to or greater than the payment standard or if the net entitlement amount would be less than $10, deny or terminate. E. Prorate the money payment for the month of application by multiplying the net entitlement amount for the entire month by the ratio of the number of days in the month including and following the date of application to 30 days. Round the result down to the nearest dollar. If the date of the application is the 31st, the payment for the month of application is 1/30 of the net entitlement amount, otherwise, the number of days may be determined by subtracting the date of application from 30 and adding 1. For example, if the date of application is February 27; 30–27=3+1=4. Multiply the net entitlement amount times 4/30. Do not use common fractions, such as ⅓ or ½. Always convert to a decimal fraction (take to four places) prior to multiplying by the net entitlement amount. F. Compute the amount due additional household members to be added after initial award by subtracting the payment standard for the current household size from the payment standard for the new household size. The difference represents the entire month’s grant amount for the month of application (application was made after the 1st of the month), prorate the additional amount due according to procedures outlined in E above using the difference in the current payment standard and the new payment standard as the grant amount. 3116 Maximum and Minimum Payments 13

body: The monthly payment cannot exceed the budget deficit nor the payment standard. No money payment can be made unless a person’s benefit would be as much as $10. Exceptions:  The adjusted money payment for a case involving recoupment or sanction from current assistance may be as little as $1.  The money payment for the month of application may be less than $10 if the full month entitlement amount is at least $10. 3120 Length of Budget Period The payment computation on ZC45 must be reviewed, printed and filed in the case record each time there is a change in the recipient’s needs or income, and at least at every review. When there is no change in need or income at the time of review, the payment computation does not change. If a manual budget is used, leave intact figures, date, worker’s name, etc., on the previous budget and make the following notations in colored pencil or ink on it: [15]  The worker’s name by the previous name.  The new date by the previous date. If the same budget is used more than once, use a different colored pencil or ink each time. Notate ZC45 as well. 3125 Trial Budget A “trial” budget is a computer or manual computation to obtain information which may have bearing on the FA budget. Any trial budget is to be maintained in the record just as any other form(s) reflecting the grant computation. A. Trial Budget at Application For applicant families with earned income work a trial budget to determine whether the applicant is eligible for the earnings disregard. 1. Persons to include in the trial budget are the same as those to include in the payment budget. 14
citation_label: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2 21

body: 2. If self-employed, subtract 40% from total proceeds to obtain gross earnings. From gross earnings subtract 20% work expense and child care expense. Subtract remainder and any unearned income from the payment standard. If there is no deficit, deny the application. 3. If the applicant has a deficit after the computation in 2 above, disregard 100% of his/her earnings for the first twelve months wages are received then deduct work expenses thereafter unless penalties apply. (See Section 3215)[16] Examples: 1. Mrs. Sorrells applies in March for herself and two children. Mrs. Sorrells works and earns $225 a month. She pays $75 a month for child care. Trial Budget $225 earned income $215 payment standard for 3 - 45 20% work expense -105 countable income $180 $110 eligible for the earnings disregard - 75 child care $105 countable income 2. Mrs. Greer applies in March for herself and two children. Mrs. Greer works and earns $385 a month. She pays $100 a month for child care. She also receives $165 a month child support. Trial Budget $385 earned income $208 countable earned income - 77 20% work expense + 165 child support $308 $373 total countable income - 100 child care $208 countable income $215 payment standard for 3 - 373 total countable income 0 assistance unit ineligible for earnings disregard and FA B. Trial Budget to Determine Contribution of Spouse of FA Child 15
citation_label: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2 22

body: Procedures in this section apply to the spouse of a dependent child living in the household. If the spouse has no income, or is receiving SSI and/or supplementation, a trial budget is not necessary, as he is receiving assistance in his own right. Likewise, if a dependent of the spouse receives FA, SSI and/or supplementation, such dependent is not to be included in the trial budget. Include both the spouse and the FA child who do not receive FA, SSI or SUP. Budget countable monthly income of the spouse only. Countable income is defined as all earned and unearned gross income as used for FA purposes. Income listed in Section 3230 may be excluded unless the exclusion is limited to applicants/recipients of FA. To determine income to be budgeted, compute as follows:  Subtract 20% from the gross earned income for work expense.  Subtract child care on an as paid basis.[17]  Add the remaining earned income to any unearned income. Then divide the countable net income by the total number of dependents to include the spouse, the FA child and their dependents, if any. “Dependent” for trial budget purposes includes the couple’s legal children under age 21, as well as other individuals, living in the same home that the couple claims, or could claim as dependents for determining Federal personal income tax liability. The result will be income to be deemed. Example: Ms. Ward receives FA for herself and three children. Her oldest child, Susie age 15, gets married and her husband moves into the home. Susie’s husband has earnings of $125 a month. Deeming Budget FA Budget $125 Susie’s husbands earnings $245 payment standard for 4 - 25 20% work expense - 50 deemed income $100 divided by 2 = $50 deemed $195 FA amount income C. Treatment of Income of Individuals Excluded from the FA Unit[18] 1. If the individual to be excluded under 3105-C.1 is the parent, stepparent or spouse of a child(ren) in the assistance unit or is a parent or stepparent disqualified under 3105-C.7 or 8[72], his/her income is deemed to the assistance unit. (See 3215-B for allowable earned income work expenses deductions.) (This provision does not apply to SSI, FCMP or SUP recipients.) 2. If the individual to be excluded is a disqualified alien under Section 3105-C.1.e and is the parent or spouse of a child(ren) or spouse of the grantee in the assistance unit, the income of that individual is considered available to his/her child(ren) and spouse. Trial Budget to Determine Contributions of an Alleged Father Living in the Home with a Child in the Assistance Unit and the Child’s Mother When the alleged father (see Glossary for definition) is living in the home with a child in the assistance unit and the child’s mother, work a trial budget according to procedures in this section to determine what amount, if any, of the alleged father’s income is to be deemed to the assistance unit. The applicant/recipient is responsible for providing verification of information needed for the trial budget. Accept verification for whatever length of time is provided. If for less than a month or more than a month, convert to a monthly amount according to procedures in Section3205-B. No income from the alleged father is to be deemed to the assistance unit and a trial budget is not necessary when: (a) the alleged father has no income, as verified under usual procedures; or (b) when the alleged father receives SSI or SUP as he is receiving assistance in his own right. Trial budget procedures are as follows: Budget countable monthly income of the alleged father. Countable income is defined as all earned and unearned gross income as used for FA purposes. Income listed in Section3230 may be excluded unless the exclusion is limited to applicants/recipients of FA. To determine income to be budgeted, compute as follows: - Subtract 20% from the gross earned income for work expense - Subtract child care on an as paid basis when paid for the child of the alleged father. Deduction not allowed if mother in home is not working or in training. - Subtract court ordered child support and/or the cost of medical support on an as paid basis for his legal children not living in the home. - Subtract court ordered alimony on an as paid basis. - Subtract the payment standard for one to represent the needs of the alleged father and deem the remainder of the income to the assistance unit up to a maximum of $165 if he is the alleged father of one child in the unit; $190 for two; $215 for three, etc. Examples: 17

body: B. Income Received Other Than Monthly 1. Weekly, bi-weekly, or semi-monthly income is to be converted to a monthly amount as follows: a. For prospective grant computations and trial budget computations, convert income to a monthly amount as follows: (1) Income anticipated to be received weekly will be multiplied by 4.3; (2) Income received every two weeks (bi-weekly) will be multiplied by 2.15. st th (3) Income received semi-monthly (twice per month, usually on the 1 and 15 ) will be multiplied by 2 and; (4) Income received at other intervals (infrequent/irregular) will be multiplied by the number of times it is anticipated to be received during the month. Each source of income must be converted separately. Averaging is limited to determining a weekly amount of income from which to derive the monthly amount. Only income, hours, hourly rates, etc., that can reasonably be anticipated to be received are to be included in the averaging. NOTE: Conversion of income is appropriate only if all income in the month will be anticipated (i.e., no actual income is used), the anticipated income will remain the same, and will be received in the same frequency. Therefore, income must not be converted for the initial month(s). b. For an overpayment computation for a month when the only verification of wage information available (company bankrupt or otherwise out of business, client or employer won’t verify, etc.) is DIR wage or SSA Bendex BEER match for the quarter, prorate the income shown over the quarter as if received equally in each month. On the notification to the client, advise of the method used to determine monthly income and that if inaccurate, proper verification must be submitted. If more accurate information is later received either from the recipient or through the Attorney General’s Welfare Fraud Unit, computations must be recalculated based on the new information. Contributions Contributions made to a member of the FA unit, which total $100.00 or less per special occasion of a birthday or high school graduation, are to be disregarded. To meet the criteria of a contribution to be disregarded, the cash gift must be received during the period in which the special occasion of a birthday or high school graduation occurs, and must total $100.00 or less per recipient per special occasion. For FA purposes, “during the period in which the special occasion occurs” includes receipt during the month before, the month of, or the month after the special occasion of a birthday or high school graduation. 1. The amount of a contribution in excess of $100.00 per recipient per special occasion of a birthday or high school graduation is to be budgeted as unearned income. Contributions which do not represent a gift for the special occasion of birthday or high school graduation cannot be disregarded under this policy and are to be considered as unearned income in the month received. 2. Contributions received from different sources can be disregarded as long as they are given for the special occasion of a birthday or high school graduation, the total amount received does not exceed $100.00 per special occasion per assistance unit member, and the contributions are received within the specified time frame. 3. Contributions from a deemer (senior parent) are treated in the same manner under this policy as contributions from other sources. However, budget the contribution (after application of this policy) or the amount deemed to the assistance unit (as determined according to 3125-E), whichever is greater. 4. A gift from an absent legal parent under court order to pay child support is child support and is treated according to 3205-E and 3210-M.[24] 24

body: E. Child Support 1. Applicants The amount of child support to be used in determining prospective eligibility or the level of benefit retrospectively must be the amount received (or anticipated to be received) each month. To determine this amount, child support is considered received (or anticipated to be received) by the applicant as follows: a. As of the day of collection, that is the date on which the payment is received by the central collection agent. and/or b. As of the date the payment is received by the applicant from the state or directly from an absent parent under court order.[25] Example 1: Client applies for FA for herself and two children in February. The children receive a total of $300.00 per month in court ordered child support. Budget is worked for February and March counting the child support received in February and the amount anticipated to be received in March. Case is determined prospectively ineligible and is denied.[26] Example 2: Same as above except children receive a total of $100.00 per month in court ordered child support. Budget is worked for February and March counting the child support received in February and the amount anticipated to be received in March. Case is prospectively eligible. Child support received in February is budgeted in April payment.[27] 2. Recipients The amount of child support used in determining prospective eligibility or the level of benefit retrospectively must be the amount mailed to the client from the State Office or the amount received directly from an absent parent under court order and not turned in each month.[28] If the child support payments have been going to the state for a case which becomes ineligible for a month, child support payment(s) for the month of ineligibility will be sent directly to the 25

Earned Income Received on a Contractual or Intermittent Basis 1. Contractual Income Prorate the total gross income of applicants/recipients to be received on a contractual basis over the number of months in the contract to arrive at a monthly gross income amount. Example: Twelve (12) month contract for $7260 would be $605 a month, even though the person was paid nine monthly payments of $806.66 each. The income is to be prorated over the number of months in the contract regardless of the fact that it may be received in fewer months than the contract covers. This applies whether the employee chooses to receive the income in fewer months than the contract covers or whether it is paid in fewer months at the convenience of the employer. 2. Intermittent Income Prorate the total gross intermittent income of self-employed applicants/recipients which may be received quarterly, semi-annually, or yearly over the period covered by the income. An example of this type income is seasonal income. For instance, a self-employed applicant/recipient works two seasons covering six months of the year and income for this work is received quarterly reflecting the total annual income. The total wages are then prorated over 12 months even though the work was completed in six months. Intermittent income received by applicants/recipients who are not self-employed is budgeted on an as received basis in the appropriate month. NOTE: Disregards are allowed for only those months in which they are applicable, work expense deductions and dependent care costs are allowed for the number of months in which the work is actually performed; however, these amounts are prorated over the number of months in the contract or the period covered by intermittent income for the self-employed. For example, using income for 12 months paid in nine months. Total gross income - 40% operating expense if self-employed difference - 20 work expense difference divided by 12 = monthly income - prorated child care (monthly child care x 9/12 amount to be budgeted 26

body: 3210 Definitions of Terms used in Dealing with Income For FA purposes “income” refers to cash or in-kind benefits usually received on a recurring basis. A. Gross Earned Income: 1. In the case of wages, commissions, or fees, the total amount of income received before any deductions, including garnished wages. 2. In the case of income from self-employment, the total profit from the enterprise after deducting the self-employment operating expense (40%). [29] B. Net Income: The difference between gross earned income and deductible work expenses. C. Earned Income: Income in cash which a person receives by his own efforts, including managerial responsibilities. It may be received in the form of wages, salaries, commissions, etc., or through self-employment. This definition includes: 1. Wages under WIA. 2. AmeriCorps living allowances from all AmeriCorps programs other than AmeriCorps * VISTA. 3. Earnings under Title I of the Elementary and Secondary Education Act. 4. Salaries of Participants in JOBS programs. 5. Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) payments. 6. Temporary worker’s compensation payments that are made to an individual who remains employed during recuperation from a temporary illness or injury pending his return to the job. 7. Severance Pay[30] D. Unearned Income: Income received without activities involving the individual’s own efforts. Examples: Contributions, unemployment compensation, Social Security benefits. E. Earnings Disregard: Exclusion of earned income by disregard of all earnings for the first twelve months of receipt of income. 27

body: F. Operating Expense: Business expenses (40% of total proceeds) deducted from income from self-employment to determine profit or gross income. G. Work Expense: The first 20% of gross earnings and the cost (on an as paid basis) of child care or care for an incapacitated adult (parent/stepparent) living in the same home receiving FA. The first 20% of gross earnings and the cost (on an as paid basis) of child care or care for an incapacitated adult (parent/stepparent) living in the same home receiving FA. H. Full-time Student: A student who is attending a school (including receiving home schooling of which the county Board of Education has been notified), college or university, Job Corps, or a course of vocational or technical training designed to fit him for gainful employment and has a school schedule equal to at least ½ of a full-time curriculum. This definition of a full-time student does not include attending GED classes.[31] I. Part-time Student: The same as a full-time student, except that his school schedule is less than ½ of a full-time curriculum and he must be employed less than full-time. J. Full and Part-time Employment 1. Full-time employment: No less than 40 hours per week at no less than the legal minimum wage rate, or, in the absence of a legal wage rate, at not less than the prevailing rate in the community. 2. Part-time employment: Any number of hours less than that listed in “1” above. NOTE: Consider that self-employed persons are working the number of hours they are at their place of business and ready to serve their customers or clients. Also consider that providing room and board is full-time employment and providing rooms only is part-time employment. K. Deemed Income: Income available to the assistance unit from any person(s) who is in the home and not in the budget who by definition is legally responsible for a member of the assistance unit (also includes alien sponsor). This income, (following trial budgeting procedures) regardless of the original source, earnings and/or unearned income, is by definition unearned income. L. Contributions for Special Occasions Contributions of $100.00 or less per recipient as a gift for the special occasion of a birthday or high school graduation. 28

Example: An individual who does housekeeping is considered self-employed if she is “in the business” of housekeeping. 2. Evidence of self-employment may include (not inclusive):  a license or permit to sell goods or services for money;  bonding;  an employer number for income tax purposes;  income tax returns filed as self-employed;  advertisements (including word-of-mouth) as being available for a particular service (i.e., housekeeping);  payment of one’s own social security tax rather than employer paying for it 3215 Earned Income Deductions and Penalties Under state and federal laws, all currently available net income of a person must be considered in determining his/her payment unless specifically exempt. A. Operating Expenses Deduct 40% of total proceeds received from self-employment to determine the profit/gross income from self-employment. B. Work Expenses The following work expenses are deducted from each applicant’s/recipient’s gross income except as specified in Section E below. 1. 20% of gross earnings. 30

body: 2. Necessary expenses for the care of each dependent child or incapacitated adult (parent/stepparent) living in the same home and receiving assistance to permit a parent to leave home and work, provided it is verified that such expenses are paid by the parent included in the budget. Dependent care expenses may not be deducted when paid to a care provider (child or adult) who is otherwise in the assistance unit or to a parent of a child in the budget. When the payment is disallowed, count only the parent’s gross income. Do not consider any income to the child (or other provider) in the assistance unit as there has been no reduction of disposable income within the assistance unit. In some instances, the cost of child care will be paid by the agency as a service cost. When all or a part of the child care is not being paid under service provisions, that part of the cost verified as paid by the working person in the budget is deductible in arriving at net earnings, so long as the day care facility is not charging additional fees over and above those set by the department. Day care facilities may charge additional fees only for optional items or services, such as for liability insurance on the child or transportation to and from the facility except when transportation services are available under a Title XX Contract. C. Disregards 1. Disregard all earned income of any child receiving assistance provided the child is a full-time student who is not full time employed (see Section 3210 for appropriate definitions); and 2. Disregard 100% of earnings from new employment in the first twelve months that wages are received.[33] Applicants are eligible for the disregard if wages are timely and accurately reported and the applicant trial budget shows a deficit (see Section 3125-A). Current recipients are eligible for the disregard if wages are timely and accurately reported. Spouses of current recipients who become applicants in the assistance unit due to recent marriage or reconciliation are eligible for the disregard without the need for the applicant trial budget if the marriage/reconciliation and wages are timely and accurately reported. Ineligibility of the individual receiving the disregard or case closure for any reason results in the end of the disregard period. [34] 31

body: Exception: Spouses of grantees receiving the disregard may continue the previously established disregard period at application without the need for the applicant trial budget provided (1) the case in which the disregard was established closed solely due to absence of the grantee because of (2) (3) death, separation, divorce or desertion the absence of the grantee was reported timely and there is no lapse in eligibility.[35] Example: The grantee in a case leaves home on February 12th and does not plan to return. The spouse of the grantee was working and receiving the disregard. The spouse reports the grantee’s absence on February 14th and applies in her own name. The absent grantee’s case is terminated th st effective February 28 . The spouse’s case is awarded effective March 1 and the previously established disregard is continued without the need for the applicant trial budget.[36] Ineligibility later discovered also results in the end of the disregard. However, for cases that are currently otherwise eligible, the previously established period may be continued (or a new one established following usual procedures). If the case is determined to be eligible in the month of discovery, work a trial budget following procedures at Section 3125-A for the month following the last month of ineligibility. If the trial budget shows a deficit, eligibility for the previously established period is re-established and may continue. Months of ineligibility count as months of the disregard.[37] Example 1: A recipient is working and eligible for the disregard. The client began receiving Unemployment Benefits in June, the third month of disregard for wages, and did not report the UC. The worker discovers in September (month of discovery) that the client had received UC and was ineligible for June, July and August. The UC ended in August. The worker processes the overpayment and works a trial budget for September (the month following the last month of ineligibility). The trial budget shows a deficit. Eligibility for the previously established disregard is re-established and may continue. June, July and August count as months of the disregard making September the sixth month of disregard.[38] Example 2: A recipient is working and eligible for the disregard. S/he began receiving RSDI in February, the 5th month of disregard, and did not report it. The RSDI ended in March. The client lost her job in April and was last paid in April. The wages received in April do not cause ineligibility. She reported all changes in June when she came in to report getting a new job that started in June. The EW works a trial budget for June (month of discovery). There is no deficit. Since the client is not currently eligible, eligibility for the previously established disregard cannot be re-established. The case is terminated effective June 30th. The worker processes the overpayment for February, March and April.[39] 32

body: Example 3: Same as above, except there is a deficit in June. The client is eligible for a new disregard period since there has been a break in employment and wages for at least one month. The EW then works a trial budget for April to determine if the previous disregard period can be re­ established. There is a deficit for April. The wages received in April can be disregarded. The worker processes an overpayment for February and March. [40] NOTE: Individuals receiving assistance after 60 months are not eligible for the twelve month earnings disregard (even if timely and accurately reported) and must be terminated as soon as possible after becoming employed, but no later than the end of the month following the month of employment. It is not necessary to work a budget in these cases as ineligibility is due to being employed, not the amount of earnings. [41] nd th Example 1: Client applies on June 2 . She goes to work on June 13 , first receives wages on June 15th and timely and accurately reports. EW works a trial budget and determines that the client is eligible for the twelve month disregard of earnings. The case is awarded in July with an effective date of June 2nd. Wages are disregarded June through May of the next year which are the first twelve months that wages are received.[42] Example 2: Recipient goes to work on May 10th and timely and accurately reports wages which were first received on May 15th. She is eligible for the earnings disregard. Wages will be disregarded May through April of the next year. [43] Example 3: Recipient goes to work on January 10th and timely and accurately reports wages which were first received on January 20th. She is eligible for the earnings disregard. The factory for which she works is closed for the entire month of March due to lack of demand for product. Workers remain employed during the shut-down. She does not receive any wages in March. Factory reopens in April and client receives wages in April which are expected to continue. Wages are disregarded in January, February and April through January of the following year. The disregard is available for each instance of new employment that is timely and accurately reported as long as there has been a break in wages and a break in employment of at least one calendar month. The break in wages and employment may be for the same month or for two different months which are consecutive. A break in wages and employment does not have to occur for the disregard to apply to new wages reported timely and accurately if the disregard has not been previously received due to penalty for failure to report wages from the previous employment timely and accurately. Example 4: Recipient goes to work on April 15th and timely and accurately reports wages which were first received April 20th. She is eligible for the earnings disregard. She loses her job on April nd th th 22 and last receives wages on April 30 . She gets another job on May 25 and timely and 33

body: accurately reports wages which were first received June 2nd. She has had a break in wages of at least one month (May) but no break in employment of at least one month. She is not eligible for a new period of earnings disregard. Wages will be disregarded in April and June of the current year through April of the following year. Example 5: Recipient goes to work in April and timely and accurately reports wages which were first received in April. She is eligible for the earnings disregard. She loses her job in April and last receives wages in May. She gets another job in June and timely and accurately reports wages which were first received in June. She has had a break in employment of at least one month (May) but no break in wages of at least one month. She is not eligible for a new earnings disregard period. Wages will be disregarded April through March of the following year. th th Example 6: Client applies on April 12 . She goes to work on April 16 and is first paid in April and timely and accurately reports. EW works trial budget and determines that she is eligible for the earnings disregard. Case is awarded in May with an effective date of April 12th. She loses job in May and last receives wages in May. Wages for April and May are disregarded in April and May. She goes to work again in July and timely and accurately reports. She is eligible for a new period of earnings disregards as she has had a break in wages and employment for at least one calendar month. Wages from new employment will be disregarded July through June of the following year. Example 7: Recipient goes to work on July 3rd and timely and accurately reports wages which were first received on July 10th. She loses this job in July and is last paid from this job in July. She goes to work again on July 15th but fails to timely and accurately report wages which were first received on July 20th. Wages from the first job are disregarded in July. Wages from the second job cannot be disregarded. Example 8: Client’s wages are being disregarded September through August of the following year. In November FA is terminated at client’s request as she plans to move out of the state. The client quits her job in November and is last paid in November. In January, the client applies for FA in Alabama and has been hired back at the same job she quit in November when she moved out of state. An applicant trial budget must be worked to determine eligibility for the disregard. If there is a deficit, the client is eligible for a new disregard period as there has been a break in employment and wages for at least one calendar month. Earnings will be disregarded January through December.[44] Example 9: Clients wages are being disregarded September through August. Client goes on maternity leave in October and receives last wages in October. Client remains employed but has no earnings. Client returns to work in January and begins receiving wages in January. Since client has not had a break in employment, she is not eligible for a new disregard period. Wages are disregarded for September and October and January through October.[45] 34

body: Code ZC24 with a “Y” in field “Is Income Excluded?” “FA?” when 100% of earnings entered on this screen are to be disregarded in determining FA eligibility. Set a C760 alert for the twelfth month of the 100% disregard period. In the C760 alert month change the “Y” in field “Is Income Excluded?” “FA?” on ZC24 to “N”. Enter the amount of child care expense, if any, on ZC17. [46] Review ZC45. If the case is ineligible, terminate the case effective the end of the 12th month of 100% disregard. If the case remains eligible make a change disposition on ZC58.[47] D. Income to Which the Earnings Disregard Does Not Apply The earnings disregard is not applicable to earned income not timely and accurately reported (defined in 3210-N), or to the earnings of an individual receiving FA beyond the 60th month due to a time limit exception criteria; or to the earnings of an individual who is excluded from the assistance unit for a reason listed in Section 3105-C. E. Penalties and Related Good Cause Circumstances Certain client activities related to earnings will result in loss of the earned income work deductions for at least one month and loss of the earned income disregard unless good cause for the activity is established. Information in this section provides the good cause circumstances related to specific actions as well as the penalties when good cause is not applicable or is not established. Apply the “prudent person principle” in evaluating reported circumstances for voluntarily terminating employment or reducing earnings or failing to make a timely and accurate report of a change in earnings. Accept the client’s statement regarding his circumstances unless there is reason to question it. Follow usual procedures in resolving incomplete/inconsistent information. 1. Good Cause Circumstances a. Good Cause for Voluntarily Terminating Employment or Reducing Earnings (1) lack of adequate child/dependent care (2) lack of transportation 35

body: (3) personal illness or illness within immediate family (4) hazardous working conditions (5) deferral from work activities by the JOBS unit (6) distance from a work or training site of more than two hours commuting time, exclusive of time necessary to transport children to and from a child care facility unless the normal round trip commuting time in the area is more than two hours b. Good cause for failing to make a timely and accurate report of receipt of earnings or change in earnings exists when the client is involved in emergencies, such as serious illness/injury, fire, natural disasters, or dangerous weather conditions which could reasonably prevent a person from making a timely report, or tragedies, such as death or serious injury to an immediate family member.[48] 2. Removal of the Earnings Disregard, Work Expense and Dependent Care Costs The activities listed below will result in removal of the above deductions from earned income on a month-to-month basis as long as the activity requiring the penalty continues to exist as follows unless good cause can be established: a. An individual voluntarily terminated his employment or reduced his earnings without good cause. This includes an applicant who terminated his job or reduced earnings within 30 days preceding the application month. The initial determination of eligibility for an applicant who reduced earnings would reflect the penalty. For a recipient who reduced earnings, the penalty is imposed as soon as possible. The penalty is to be applied each month until: (1) the client has regained/obtained employment/earnings comparable to those which were terminated or reduced; or (2) the job/higher earnings from the particular employer are no longer available. (If the person has not regained/obtained comparable employment/earnings and the job/higher earnings are not available as verified by the employer or former employer, the penalty is no longer applied.) 36

body: Example: Ms. Jones reduced her earnings from the Acme Company without good cause. The penalty is invoked and the July payment is reduced. For the August payment, it is verified that the additional hours at the Acme Company are no longer available as someone else has been hired. Since the hours are not available, the penalty is no longer applicable. (If the hours are available and Ms. Jones does not have other comparable employment/earnings, the penalty continues for the next month.) b. A recipient failed without good cause to make a timely and accurate report (see 3210-N) of receipt of earnings or a change in earnings. The earnings disregard is no longer available. Loss of the work expense and child care deduction is applied on a month-to-month basis. [49] Example 1: The client reports on March 10th that she began working part-time on February 10th at $10 a day once a week. Because of failure to timely report, the client is not eligible for the disregard. Additionally the worker must impose penalties on February and March income. The penalty will be applied retroactively as part of the overpayment computation for February and March. On March 10th, the worker will determine eligibility prospectively for April allowing earned income deductions. If eligibility is established, payment for April is determined prospectively allowing earned income deductions. Example 2: The client reports on December 10th that she received a 40 cent raise in her hourly wages beginning in November. December is the client’s 4th month of earnings disregard. Because of failure to timely report, the client loses all months of the earnings disregard, and the earned income deductions for November and December. For November and December the penalty will be applied retroactively as part of the overpayment computation. On December 10th the worker will determine eligibility prospectively for January allowing the earned income deductions. If eligibility is established, payment for January is determined prospectively allowing only the earned income deductions. The earnings disregard cannot be applied to earnings received January through August.[50] 3225 Unearned Income to be Budgeted All unearned income received by any member of the budget group or received by someone not in the budget but in the home and designated for a member of the budget group must be included in the budget, unless specifically excluded or disregarded under provisions in Section 3230. For example, when Social Security benefits are paid to a representative payee on behalf of a member of the assistance unit and the payee lives in the same household as the assistance unit, the income must be counted in whole. When the representative payee does not live in the household, the Social Security is included only to the extent that a payee makes it available for the support of the beneficiary. Likewise; 37

A windfall might come from an inheritance, dog track, bingo or lottery winnings, personal injury awards, insurance settlements, worker compensation awards, etc. The amount to be budgeted is that which is not earmarked and used for the purpose for which it is paid, i.e., monies for back medical bills resulting from accidents or injury, funeral and burial costs, replacement or repair of items covered in an insurance settlement. body: [1] Chapter 10 Supplementation Eligibility Process 10000 Introduction The county Department of Human Resources is directly responsible for administering the public aid programs and making the decisions in regard to each person seeking aid. All decisions must be in keeping with laws governing the programs and with rules made by the State Department of Human Resources. The staff must, therefore, be thoroughly familiar with all legal requirements, policies and regulations relating to eligibility. Prior to January 1, 1974, the state administered the adult category public assistance programs partially funded with Federal monies under the Social Security Act. These programs, Old Age Pension (OAP), Aid to the Blind (AB), and Aid to the Permanently and Totally Disabled (APTD) were modified after January 1, 1974 to provide supplementary assistance payments from state funds alone for certain aged, blind and disabled persons. A. Supplemental Security Income Public Law 92-603 amended Title XVI of the Social Security Act to provide a federal program of supplemental security income (SSI) for aged, blind and disabled people with limited income and resources. Under this law, the Social Security Administration (SSA) began making payments January 1, 1974 to most recipients of the former OAP, APTD and AB programs of the Department of Human Resources. The SSI payment for many of the OAP, APTD, and AB recipients was equal to or more than their DHR payment. SSI benefits for some recipients, including most couple recipients and some individual recipients with additional shelter costs and/or special needs, were less than their DHR payments, and some former recipients were not eligible for SSI. B. Mandatory Supplementation To insure that persons would not be financially disadvantaged under SSI, Public Law 93-66 was passed to require that states implement supplemental programs to: (1) provide for payments to all December 1973 recipients of OAP, APTD and AB whose income level under SSI during any month (CIL – Current income level) is 2
citation_label: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2 57

metadata.title: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2

body: less than their income level as of December 1973 (MIL – Minimum income level); (2) make payments equal to at least the difference between the December 1973 (3) income level and the income level under SSI; and maintain the December 1973 income level, reducing it only as a decrease in needs would have caused a difference in the December 1973 payment had such decrease been reported then. To identify persons subject to provisions of PL 93-66, show the amount of the MIL or zeros, as appropriate, on ZC26, SUP-Member Information, screen. The MIL is the total of amount of budgeted income/resources plus amount of payment for December 1973. If it is known that the individual was a recipient in December 1973, but case record material for that time has been destroyed, use one of the amounts below in the MIL, according to the category under which the person received aid in December 1973 and whether in his home or in a nursing home: Own Home Nursing Home AB $ 230 $ 32 APTD 232 22 OAP 258 48 C. When an individual who received AB, APTD, or OAP in December 1973, becomes ineligible for optional supplementation, terminate according to usual advance notice and accrual rights procedures. Then compare his current income level (CIL) to his minimum income level (MIL). If the CIL is less than the MIL, send the case record to the state department, Attention: Family Assistance Division, as eligibility determinations for persons eligible for only mandatory supplementation are the responsibility of the staff of that division. D. Optional Supplementation In addition to required or mandatory supplementation, the law gave states the option to make payments to aged, blind, and disabled persons under a program of optional supplementation. In accordance with these provisions, optional payments are made in Alabama to eligible individuals who were not receiving assistance in December 1973. Optional supplementation payments are provided to individuals who require nursing care and who are: 1. SSI recipients living in their own home or foster home licensed or approved by the Department of Human Resources; or 3

NOTE: This includes SSI SUP recipients who became ineligible for SSI due to income on or before March 7, 1986 and would have continued eligible as a Non-SSI SUP recipient except for the program change whereby payments would only be made to SSI recipients who were otherwise eligible; or 3. “Grandfathered” SSI and Non-SSI recipients living in their own home or a personal care home, hospital or foster home licensed or approved by the Department of Human Resources who were receiving SUP payments for personal care as of September 30, 1986. These individuals will continue to receive supplementation payments for as long as they remain continuously eligible under supplementation eligibility criteria in effect prior to October 1, 1986. A break in eligibility for any reason results in loss of grandfathered status and the case must be terminated unless the individual now qualifies for a nursing care supplement. Optional supplementation payments are also made to persons receiving care in a cerebral palsy treatment center (only for persons who were eligible for such payment in May 1981). 10001 Summarized Eligibility Requirements For the worker’s convenience, general conditions of eligibility applicable to persons applying for supplementation on or after October 1, 1986 are summarized below. Detailed policies and procedures are given elsewhere. Refer to Section 12010-B for handling of applications. To receive aid under one of the three categories of supplementation, in addition to being a resident of Alabama, a person must be receiving: A. SSI (Aged, blind, disabled); B. Medicaid waiver services at the skilled nursing facility level. (Level to be determined by the state office Family Assistance Division); and; 4

The applicant/recipient must pay the provider of this care. 10002 Supplemental Security Income Status An individual’s status with SSA for SSI benefits must be cleared before s/he can apply for supplementation under OAP, APTD or AB. If an individual is ineligible for SSI benefits for any reason, s/he is not eligible for supplementation. 1. If a person is receiving SSI, accept his application and process it to a determination of eligibility or ineligibility. 2. If a person was denied SSI or becomes ineligible for SSI for any reason, s/he is not eligible for a supplementation payment and an application must not be accepted (or assistance must be terminated.) 10015 Entitlement to Medicaid Due to Receipt of Cash Assistance in (Non-SSI) SUP An eligible recipient of mandatory, mandatory/optional or optional supplementation (Non-SSI) is entitled to Medicaid for as long as s/he receives a supplementary payment under AB, APTD, or OAP. Certification of Medicaid eligibility for eligible recipients of non-SSI SUP is accomplished each month by the sharing of all active cases in the base file between the State Department of Human Resources and the Alabama Medicaid Agency. The information in the base file is updated as indicated by FACETS. 10030 Entitlement to Medicaid Due to Grandfathering Legislation or Court Decisions Federal legislation, regulations and court cases have provided protection of Medicaid benefits for individuals who would otherwise be ineligible for Medicaid benefits or lose entitlement to Medicaid benefits under the circumstances given below. Medicaid entitlement under these provisions exists as long as the individual meets the specified criteria. 5

body: 10032 Persons in Medical Institutions Follow procedures below in determining and certifying Medicaid eligibility of “grandfathered” nursing home cases. “Grandfathered” refers to Medicaid eligibility of a person who was eligible for Medicaid as a patient in a nursing home on December 31, 1973. As a nursing home patient, he was eligible either as a money payment or no- money payment recipient. He continues eligible for “grandfathered” Medicaid for as long as he remains in the nursing home, has a need for the care provided, and receives a mandatory supplementation payment or would receive a money payment except for income sufficient to meet his personal needs but not in excess of certain income levels. His “grandfathered” status is not affected by a reclassification from receiving money payment to receiving no money payment, or vice versa, however, the procedure for determining and certifying eligibility is affected by such classification as set out below. A. Mandatory money payment recipient – See Section 12012. B. No money payment – The Medicaid District Office (MDO) is responsible for re­ determining eligibility of “grandfathered in” nursing home patients whose income is sufficient to meet their needs while in the nursing home, but is not as high as the following levels: OAP - $258 or $262 with RSDI APTD - $222 or $230 with RSDI AB - $225 The MDO will determine eligibility based on SSI criteria. However, if a “grandfathered” nursing home patient is not eligible based on those criteria, he is entitled to another determination using eligibility policies and procedures in effect prior to January 1974, including provision for disregard of the 20% RSDI increase of August 1972. This determination is made by the Family Assistance Division upon referral from the MDO. If the person continues eligible, MDO will continue to certify him as an active MDO case. 6

body: 10034 Persons who would be Eligible for Supplementation Except for the RSDI Cost-of-Living Increases Occurring After April 1977 Public Law 94-566 (The Pickle Amendment) provides for Medicaid coverage for any recipient of Old Age Pension, Aid to the Blind and Aid to the Permanently and Totally Disabled who: (1) Received RSDI and SUP payments concurrently after April 1977; (2) Currently receives RSDI; (3) Is not eligible for SUP due to income; and (4) Would be eligible for SUP if all RSDI cost-of-living increases received (by him or his ineligible spouse) since he last received RSDI and SUP concurrently were deducted from current RSDI benefits. Only cost-of-living increases, not other RSDI increases, are deductible. However, as the Department of Human Resources is no longer making optional SUP payments to non-SSI SUP recipients, this provision becomes obsolete except as indicated in A below: A. Persons Eligible 1. Grandfathered Medicaid Recipients A person certified under this provision as of March 7, 1986 is grandfathered for continued Medicaid eligibility as long as he remains otherwise continuously eligible under this provision; that is, he would be eligible for SUP due to income if SUP payments were made to non-SSI recipients. If he becomes ineligible for Medicaid for any reason, his grandfathered status is lost. 2. Grandfathered Non-SSI SUP Recipients Grandfathered non-SSI SUP recipients whose SUP payment is terminated because of an RSDI cost-of living adjustment (COLA) will continue eligible for Medicaid under this provision. Detailed instructions for Medicaid only certification are issued as part of the mass change instructions specific to the COLA. B. Financial Requirements 1. Determine the amount(s) of applicable cost-of-living increases; increases for other reasons, such as a change from wife’s to widow’s benefits or a recalculation due to additional covered employment, are not to be included. 7

body: 2. Subtract the total of all cost-of-living increases received by the Medicaid only or non-SSI SUP recipient since he last received SUP and RSDI from his current RSDI benefits to derive countable RSDI for Medicaid only. Also, subtract the ineligible spouse’s cost-of-living increases for the same period of time from her current RSDI in determining the amount of income to be deemed. 3. Work a trial budget according to procedures in Chapter 12 using countable RSDI from “2” above. If the recipient’s income exceeds need, he is no longer/not Medicaid eligible. If his income does not exceed need, he is/remains eligible for Medicaid only. C. Medicaid Certification Procedures The current amount of RSDI received by the individual is to be entered on ZC30 at “Social Security (RSDI)” and the amount of disregarded RSDI is also entered on ZC30 at “Disregarded RSDI”. Certify Medicaid by completing an award disposition on ZC49. 1. If the individual has a spouse who is also eligible for Medicaid only under these provisions, the spouse’s own RSDI benefit amount is to be entered on ZC30. Certify Medicaid for the spouse on ZC49. 2. If the individual has an ineligible spouse, determine the amount of deemed income to be shown on ZC30 at “Contribution/Individual” using the ineligible spouse’s current RSDI benefit less any cost-of-living increases received after the eligible spouse last received SUP and RSDI. D. Redetermination Process Re-determine eligibility for Medicaid at least every 12 months by completing/updating FACETS screens and, when appropriate, other needed Forms such as 696, 797, etc. Once a recipient is determined eligible for Medicaid under these provisions, all subsequent cost-of-living increases are to be disregarded in determining continued eligibility. Terminate Medicaid when the individual is no longer eligible. 10100 Determining Initial and Continuing Eligibility Responsibilities for the determination of initial eligibility for nursing care supplementation cases are shared by eligibility staff and Elderly and Disabled waiver 8

body: case managers. Generally, eligibility staff receives the completed application packet from the case manager either from the Department of Senior Services or the Department of Public Health. Detailed policy regarding this eligibility process is in Section 12010-B. Following the initial determination of eligibility, the determination of continuing eligibility of these cases and personal care supplementation cases is made by eligibility staff under usual procedures, policies, etc. Under rare circumstances applications may also be received directly from a Medicaid waiver recipient. Regardless of the source of the application, the eligibility record must contain proper documentation establishing eligibility. Once established, eligibility continues until a change in the recipient’s circumstances or agency policy occurs. 10105 Application Process - General The application process includes all activities from the time application is made by a “proper applicant” until the mailing of payment or other disposition of the application. If a person other than a “proper applicant” makes a request for aid for another without that person’s knowledge, the county department must advise the person making the request that the department can take no action without the knowledge and consent of the person concerned. A. Definition of Terms Used in the Application Process 1. A proper applicant is a competent adult who is physically able to apply for himself or on behalf of a minor (child under 19) or physically or mentally handicapped adult and who has made application for public assistance to the county department and whose application has not been disposed of by appropriate county department action. A proper applicant for a minor who is not in foster care or a physically or mentally handicapped adult is the person with whom he lives and who is responsible for his care, or a legal guardian, committee, or other legal representative. When the minor is mentally competent and between 16 and 19 years old with no legally appointed guardian, committee or other representative, and he is not in the care of another person, he may be the proper applicant for himself. The proper applicant for a minor who is in foster care under supervision of the county or state department is the county director or his (her) designee. The person for whom assistance is requested is referred to as the applicant or recipient and his name and Social Security number are the case identifiers. 9

body: When this person is a minor, or an adult with a legal representative or legal guardian, his assistance must be issued in the name of a substitute payee. The substitute payee for a minor in foster care is the foster parent; for a minor not in foster care, the substitute payee is the person responsible for his care. The legal representative or legal guardian is substitute payee for the person for whom his is appointed as such. 2. An application is the action taken by an individual in filling out and signing an application, Form 693, requesting assistance. The application may be new (initial) or it may be a reapplication. 3. An inquiry is a request for information only. An inquiry differs from an application in that the person’s intent is to obtain information rather than to receive financial aid. 4. A referral is classed as an inquiry. It is a request for information, service, or aid in behalf of an individual. It may be made by an agency, an institution, or another person. The county department will consider a referral as an inquiry until application is made. 5. The date of application for persons applying directly to the eligibility unit is the date on which the prescribed application form, properly signed by the applicant or a person acting responsibly for him is received in the county department. The date of application for others is the date on which the properly competed application packet is received by the eligibility worker/unit from the Medicaid waiver service e case manager. B. Clearing the Application Assure that every application and reapplication has been cleared, according to usual procedures for assuring that no duplicate file exists. A separate application and case record file is necessary for each person within a family group who may be eligible for aid in his own right. Each applicant must be individually cleared through county, FACETS and IEVS files. Refer to Sections 20000 and 20015 for instructions setting up the case record. 10

body: 10110 Standard of Promptness A. Not more than 45 days in OAP and AB, and not more than 60 days in APTD, shall elapse between the date of application and date the first payment or the notice of denial is mailed. Only the following pending reasons are acceptable for exceeding the standard of promptness: 1. Agency failure to secure needed verification after repeated attempts. To meet this requirement, all efforts to secure the verification must be documented. 2. Client’s request that application be continued beyond the standard of promptness in order to provide additional information and/or verification. 3. Failure of applicant to provide information and verification requested. 4. Administrative emergencies that could not reasonably be controlled by the agency. (Illness and staff vacancies do not meet this definition.) B. Keep to an absolute minimum the number of cases in which the standard of promptness is exceeded. The reason for any delay must be specifically recorded. C. Complete as quickly as possible the investigation on delayed applications. Record the action on the application and the reasons for doing so in the narrative. Indicate disposition on ZC58. Notify each applicant of action taken. A determination of continued eligibility must be made for every recipient at any time changes which may affect eligibility are reported by the recipient or collateral sources or when information in the case record appears inconsistent. Additionally, the agreement to provide personal care or nursing care must be validated at least every 12 months. Any time the provider changes a new agreement is required. SSI status for SSI SUP recipients and income and resource information for non-SSI SUP recipients must be re-verified at the time of the annual validation of the agreement to provide care, i.e., the C600 alert month. A. Definitions of Terms (Phrases) Used in Continuing Eligibility 1. A Recipient is any person who receives a public assistance money payment and/or is certified to the Alabama Medicaid Agency for Medicaid. 2. Determination of Continued Eligibility is an investigation to determine that a recipient continues to meet all eligibility requirements. 11

body: 3. Determination of Continued Eligibility Process refers to all of the activities involved in the investigation and determination of continuing eligibility for public assistance. B. Recipient Reporting Requirements Recipients are required to report changes in their situation which may affect eligibility or amount of payment within 10 days of the date the change occurred. Failure to report a change promptly may result in an erroneous payment. 10120 Interview for Application or Continuing Eligibility At least one face-to-face interview or telephone interview must be held with the client during the application process. The client may be assisted, if he so desires, by an individual(s) of his choice in the various aspects of determining and/or re-determining eligibility. He may be accompanied by such individual(s) in his contacts with the agency and when so accompanied may also be represented by such individual(s). There will be instances when a client cannot be interviewed personally when eligibility is being established. In such cases, have the person responsible for his care complete the 693 or 703 and have the client complete this form and sign it as soon as possible. When someone other than the client, his legal representative, his legal guardian, or the proper applicant for a minor, completes the application form, have him leave blank the section relating to the after-death payment. A. For an applicant, obtain the following information: 1. How he has managed in the past. 2. Explanation of changes which brought him to the agency. B. Explain the following to the applicant and to the recipient as needed: 1. The eligibility requirements. 2. The agency’s standard of promptness in acting on applications. 3. A person’s right to a fair hearing and the procedure for requesting a hearing 4. The method of establishing eligibility. a. The use of documentary and other records for verifying factual data. 12

body: Revenue Service and as necessary from other agencies in Alabama and other states. C. Secure from the applicant the following: 1. A completed 693. NOTE: Application forms may be mailed out or the client may take them from the office and seek the assistance of anyone in completing them. If they are to be returned by mail, give the client a stamped envelope addressed to the agency. After the forms have been signed, any changes or additions to them must be initialed by the client and dated; otherwise, the narrative must reflect the supplementary information furnished by the client. 2. Information about third party medical insurance. Explain that third party benefits include any sick claims, street indemnity policies, disability, accident or hospital coverage or to insurance benefits, or other money benefits arising from injuries or medical care. Any Medicaid recipient is deemed under Alabama law to have assigned such insurance or benefits to the state as an eligibility requirement for Medicaid to the extent that Medicaid assistance is provided. The Alabama Medicaid Agency is defined to be the last to pay, so that any such benefits to which a Medicaid recipient is entitled must be used to pay the medical provider or to reimburse the Alabama Medicaid Agency. If a SUP recipient states he has acquired such coverage or has had a change in coverage, send the information to the Medicaid Agency via email. Print a copy of the email for the case record. 3. Information about his after-death beneficiary. The designated person may be the husband, wife, or someone 21 years of age or over. An employee of the Department of Human Resources may not serve as after-death beneficiary. If the applicant/recipient wishes to designate the operator or manager of the nursing home, he may do so only if the nursing home is a corporation. If it is not and he wants to designate the operator or manager of the nursing home, in lieu of the home itself, advise him in doing so the monies may or may not be used for the benefit of the home. If the applicant/recipient has a legal guardian or legal representative, such person may designate someone to receive the after-death payment. He may designate himself, provided he does so before the client’s death. 14

Other forms as necessary. 10125 Investigation to Secure Facts to Determine Initial or Continuing Eligibility Follow the procedures outlined below in determining eligibility. A. In preparation for the investigation, clear the files for available records on the applicant/recipient and study the material in the record to become familiar with the facts at hand. If it is necessary to contact public welfare agencies in other states, use form 1389 for routine out-of-state inquiries. B. The applicant/recipient is the most important source of information in the determination of his eligibility. Expect him to provide full and complete information (and verification, if possible) about his situation. If he cannot provide sufficient evidence/information as needed, offer help in obtaining data and securing verifications. When indicated, give the client a stamped, self-addressed envelope in which to mail the needed information. C. Analyze the information furnished by the applicant/recipient to identify gaps or inconsistencies that raise questions about eligibility. Weigh complete information in relation to agency policy to determine whether all points of eligibility are shown to be met. D. Verify receipt of SSI (or denial of individuals and/or couples) and RSDI amounts by using the SDX or BENDEX inquiry screens, in the CP alert month and other times as needed. If necessary, contact the district Social Security Administration Office for any additional information needed. When information is needed from a reference source to clarify unclear, incomplete or inconsistent information, always explain to the client what information is needed, why it is needed, how it is to be used and from whom it is to be obtained. Use as references only those sources having the facts needed at hand. When sources which do not disclose information without authorization are needed to verify facts, obtain such authorization in writing from the client. There may be times when a client objects to the use of a particular reference. When he does, consider his reasons for objecting and, if indicated, select another reference to which he agrees. The determination of eligibility for public aid is the responsibility of the county department and references are not expected to recommend that aid be approved or disapproved. 2. A person is used as a reference only of the has definite facts which he is willing to give about points of eligibility, and the county requests information related to these specific points. Examples of appropriate references are: (a) wages – the employer(s), (b) residence – neighbors, etc. 3. The reason certain information is needed. 4. The case record will include the facts the references give pertaining to eligibility and the case record will be available to the client and/or his representative as a part of the exhibits in the event of an appeal. Routine procedures in certain situations will require going beyond information on the form itself in determining eligibility and the amount of the payment, such as when professional medical examinations are needed for a determination of the need for personal care. When an applicant/recipient fails to provide information or verification within his competence, consider failure as refusal and take action as indicated in this section after following the procedures below. The case record narrative should document your attempts to obtain from the client the information or verification required. The 686, 1132 or narrative recording must show that the client knew what was required and the consequences of failure to cooperate. If the 686 is used in meeting the requirements of this section, add the specific type of adverse action to be taken if the client does not keep the appointment or does not provide the necessary information. Take action as follows to assure that the client and agency meet responsibilities for obtaining information/verification. 1. Evaluate the information and/or verification that is being required to determine if it is essential to the determination of initial or continuing eligibility. 2. Upon determining that the requested material is essential, determine if the client’s presence is required or if the client could provide the necessary documentation by letter. If client’s presence is required, follow “3” and “4”. If the client’s presence is not required, follow “5” and “6”. 3. If an interview is required, send a 686 or 1132 to the client for an appointment and list verifications to be provided. Schedule the appointment at least seven calendar days from the date the request for an interview is mailed. 4. If the client does not keep the appointment, deny the application, or process termination. 5. If the client’ presence is not required and information and/or verification from the client is necessary to determine initial and/or continuing eligibility, state the specific information needed on the 686 or 1132 and enclose a self-addressed stamped envelope in which the client may supply the information. body: 6. If the client does not furnish the information and/or verification requested, deny the application, or process termination. H. Take action within 30 days to investigate any report of a change in the client’s situation. Changes in SSI status, income and resources (for non-SSI recipients) must be verified. Do not withhold payment during the period in which the investigation is being conducted, except when the client fails to respond to agency notices, in accordance with Section G above or J below. Changes for nursing care supplementation recipients must be reported by or confirmed with the Medicaid waiver services case manager prior to action being taken. Changes for personal care SUP recipients may be reported as follows: 1. Orally – Changes may come to the attention of the worker from the client or references in person or by telephone. a. It is permissible for the worker to accept a report of change from the client by telephone provided it is recorded in the narrative and includes (1) the date the change occurred, (2) all facts pertinent to eligibility related to such change and (3) any activity needed from the client. b. When the client comes to the office to report a change in circumstances, etc., always secure a written statement from him using the 703. (See Appendix II) 2. Correspondence – A letter or note signed by the client and dated may serve as a report of change. Reports from collateral sources will not serve as a notice of change. The client must be contacted. 3. Intra-agency – Other workers in the agency may notify the eligibility worker of a change in a client’s situation. The client must be contacted unless a statement about the change was secured from him. 4. Inter-agency – Routine comparison of information on SDX, BENDEX or Medicaid screens with information in the client’s case record may serve as notice of a change in his situation. I. Process changes in supplementary payments caused by change in the client’s situation according to the following guidelines: 18

body: 1. When a change in situation is reported by a recipient who also receives SSI and the change may affect his SSI benefit, or by a non-SSI recipient who may now be eligible for SSI, tell him to advise the Social Security Administration of the change, if he has not done so already. 2. Except as specified in “3”, “4”, or “5” below, process the change immediately in line with usual advance notification procedures. If the recipient also receives SSI, the SDX screen should carry information about how his change affects his SSI benefit. When you have complete, current SSI information, process immediately any change in the supplementary payment resulting from the SSI change. Persons who become ineligible for SSI for any reason are no longer eligible for supplementation. 3. If a mandatory supplementation recipient enters a nursing home, take action as stated below: a. If the recipient gets SSI and also has other income or was denied SSI due to excess income, terminate and refer to the Medicaid Certification Division district office. b. If the recipient gets only SSI, terminate and re-award according to Section 12012-A after the SSI is reduced. c. If the client does not receive SSI for reasons other than excess income, process according to Section 12012-B. 4. If a former recipient in a nursing home returns to his own home. Process his application as usual. If he is not eligible for an optional supplement, and he received OAP, AB or APTD in December 1973, compare his current level (CIL) to his minimum income level (MIL). If his CIL is less than his MIL, mail the case record to the Public Assistance Division. 5. If an optional state supplementation recipient enters a medical facility such as a nursing home, continue benefits under the following conditions: a. A physician has certified that the person’s stay is not likely to exceed 90 days; and b. There is evidence that the person needs to continue to maintain and provide for the expense of the home or living arrangements to which s/he may return and 19
