NOTE: For SSI recipients, verification should be obtained through the Social Security Administration that unreduced SSI benefits will be continued during the temporary period of institutionalization. If the recipient has not left the facility by the end of the third month, contact the Social Security Administration to determine if SSI benefits have been reduced. If so, notify the recipient that the Optional Supplementation check must be stopped and refer to the Medicaid district office. J. Refer to alert procedures in the FCUM for controlling actions when changes which may affect eligibility or amount of payment are anticipated with reasonable certainty. When additional information is received, take action, as appropriate, to continue, change or terminate aid. If the client does not respond by the end of the alert month, process termination following usual advance notice and accrual rights policy. K. Verify income and resource information for non-SSI recipients in the month of the CP alert. 10130 Accrual Rights of Applicant/Recipient A. Applicant An applicant accrues the right to a money payment if he is alive and eligible on the day the eligibility worker completes the award documents. Then, he is entitled to a money payment for the month of award, i.e., the month during which the award document is processed. If the month of award is the same as the month of application, the payment for that month is a prorated amount equal to the amount payable if payment were made for the entire month multiplied by the ratio (carried to four decimal places) of the number of days in the month including and following the date of application to 30 days, rounded down to the nearest dollar. Applicant accrual rights are in effect until the case is actually awarded. 20

body: If an applicant is not eligible on the day the eligibility worker completes the eligibility determination (and would complete award documents if the applicant were eligible) the application must be denied and no money payment may be made, even though the applicant was eligible in one or more prior months. However, the applicant may be eligible for Medicaid according to Section 24455. B. Recipient A recipient’s right to a money payment accrues on the first day of each month provided he is alive and eligible on that date. If his needs increase after the first day of the month, his payment for the month may be increased; however, in case of payments after death, the county department must establish that the recipient was alive on the date that the increase was authorized. See Section 22840. 10135 Taking Action of Basis of Findings Evaluate all information at hand to determine if all points of eligibility are met. Process the proper documents as appropriate. A. Applicants 1. Approval – Approve an application when the eligibility record, properly documented, shows that all points of eligibility are met. NOTE: When available funds are expended on current recipients, the state department may freeze the caseload at the number of persons for whom funds are available. In the event of such an administrative emergency, specific procedural instructions to be followed during the emergency period will be released in administrative letters or official memoranda. 2. Denial – Deny an application when an applicant fails to meet one or more of the eligibility requirements, or requests that his application be discontinued. Also deny the application when the application process cannot be completed because the applicant has died or cannot be located. 3. Transfers – When an applicant moves to another county, the application is to be denied in county no. 1 (county where application was originally filed) and is then to be registered and processed to completion in county no. 2 (county to which the applicant moved). To protect the applicant’s accrual rights and 21

body: a. Determine whether any overpayments have been made and follow appropriate procedures (Chapter 22) for handling them, and b. In any case, remind the recipient again of the need for reporting a change within 10 days of the date of change and the kinds of changes which could affect eligibility and need to be reported. 2. Before action is taken to reduce or terminate aid or terminate Medicaid (except where notice was provided regarding a closed period of eligibility), refer to procedures below for providing advance notification. 3. (Refer to “C” below and to Section 10140.) 6. Termination Later Found Invalid – The procedures below are designed to minimize client and worker duplication of effort when a termination has been processed and is later found to be invalid. In applying these procedures, give careful attention to the dates when: the termination was processed, the termination was effective, the termination was found to be invalid; and to the reason for the finding that a termination is invalid. a. Hearing Request Within Advance Notice Period – Assistance must be reinstated upon a client’s filing of a hearing request, which has been accepted, within the advance notice period. No application activity is required of the client. Reinstate assistance affective the first of the month following the month in which assistance was last received. b. Termination Found Invalid Because of Agency Error – When an agency error results in termination, assistance must be reinstated upon discovery of the error. body: reinstatement is the first of the month following the month in which assistance was last received. When the time between processing the termination and processing the subsequent award is 30 days or more, review all information on the last 693, 694 and 797 with the client to assure that case record information is current. This review may be done by telephone. Document information from the telephone contact in the narrative. c. Termination Found Invalid Because of Change in Client’s Situation or a Change From the Already Reported Change – When a client-reported change results in termination and the change does not occur or another change in the client’s situation results in no loss of eligibility, whether the client must reapply or whether assistance is to be reinstated without application activity depends on when the client notified the agency of the fact that the reported change did not occur or of the other change. If the agency is notified before the effective date of termination and there was, in fact, no loss of eligibility, assistance is to be reinstated without application activity effective with the first of the month following the month in which assistance was last received. If the agency is not notified before the effective date of termination or there is, in fact, a loss of eligibility but a subsequent change seemingly results in the client regaining eligibility, the client must reapply according to usual procedures. The following examples illustrate some more common occurrences and correct procedures to be applied: Example 1: Client reports plans to move to another state later in the current month. Termination is processed to be effective the end of the current month. Client calls before the end of the current month to advise that he will not be moving as planned. Assistance is to be reinstated effective the first of the next month. Example 2: Same as example 1, except client calls on the fourth of the next month to advise that he did not move as planned or that he moved and decided to return. Client must reapply.* Example 3: Client’s C600 alert was due in July and was not completed as of the end of the month. Termination is processed for August 31st . Alert is completed before August 31. Assistance is to be reinstated effective the first of September. 24

See Section 12010-B C. Advance Notification of Action 1. Except as set out in “2” below, when aid is being terminated or reduced, advance notice must be mailed at least 10 days before the effective date of action. This provides an explanation of the action taken, the facts considered in determining the action needed, and the recipient’s right to present any additional facts he wishes considered before expiration of the advance notification period. The computer-generated FACETS notice will serve as advance notice of reduction provided a change disposition reflecting the change is processed timely. a. If a hearing has not been requested (during the 10 day advance notice period) and no additional information changing the original decision has been presented by the effective date of the action, no further action or notice of action is required. b. If a hearing has not been requested but additional information is presented either through a conference or otherwise, take the action that may be called for based on the most current information made available. c. If a hearing is requested prior to the expiration of the 10 day advance notice, continue aid at an unreduced rate by submitting a change, if proposed action was reduction, or by processing a reinstatement, if proposed action was termination. Maintain this benefit level until the hearing is held and the decision on it is released. Then take action in line with the hearing decision. 25

body: 2. It is not necessary to provide advance notice of action to be taken in situations listed below. However, the notice must be sent no later than the effective date of action, except for situations described in “a” and “d”. a. The county department has factual information confirming the death of the recipient. b. The county department receives a clear written statement signed by a recipient that s/he no longer wishes assistance, or that s/he waives the 10 day advance notice period to avoid receiving an overpayment, or that gives information which requires termination or reduction of assistance, and the recipient has indicated, in writing, that he understands that this must be the consequence of supplying such information. A clear written statement obtained from a recipient must contain the specific action to be taken on his money payment and/or Medicaid and his understanding of this action. This includes cancelling of a check returned by a recipient when he requests in writing that this be done. c. The recipient has been admitted or committed to a public institution. d. The recipient’s whereabouts are unknown and agency mail directed to him has been returned by the post office indicating no known forwarding address. The recipient’s check must, however, be made available to him if his whereabouts become known before the last day of the month covered by the returned check. e. The recipient has been accepted for assistance in another county or state and that fact has been established by the county department previously providing assistance. f. The recipient has been placed in skilled nursing care, intermediate care, or long term hospitalization. g. A special allowance granted for a specific period is terminated and the recipient has been informed in writing at the time of award that the allowance shall automatically terminate at the end of the specified period. h. The recipient has left, or has been removed from, a foster home. Whether or not he will be in another foster home is irrelevant. 26

body: In applying the above policies, careful attention should be given to the basic accrual rights policies as set out in Section 10130. D. Ex Parte Determination after Termination of Non-SSI SUP Cases (for reasons other than death). After termination of a non-SSI SUP case (for reasons other than death). mail the recipient a manual notice (PSD-657) advising of the SUP closure and possible continued Medicaid coverage through the Qualified Medicaid Beneficiary (QMB) program explaining that this coverage is administered by the local Medicaid District Office (MDO). Indicate the address and phone number of the appropriate Medicaid District Office and that information regarding their case has been forwarded to that office for further information. (See Appendix III for a listing of MDO’s.) Mail copies of the current SUP case material to the MDO with a cover letter explaining the SUP termination and potential QMB eligibility. NOTE: The administrative conversion of a non-SSI case to an SSI SUP case does not constitute a “termination” for purposes of this referral. 10140 Advising Applicant/Recipient of Action in the Case The applicant/recipient must be promptly advised of approval or denial of the application; changes in payment, including initiating or deleting protective payment; and termination of the case, except when the applicant/recipient is deceased or his whereabouts are unknown. “Promptly” means not later than the effective date of action. When a payment is reduced or terminated, the notice to the recipient must clearly and briefly state every condition or circumstance which had an impact in causing the reduction or termination. The particular Manual references including section number, Administrative Letter, or other policy issuance on which the negative action is based must also be cited. Up to three references must be cited when two or more reasons are the basis for action. A. System-Generated notices Refer to the FCUM for automated notice procedures and reason messages provided. Notices are produced by the system on the night of entry of appropriate code or data element (provided there is no error precluding file update) and are mailed from Montgomery directly to clients on the next work day. 27

body: B. Manual Notices The worker must enter the hold notice code and provide notice of action using Form 657 or 660, as appropriate, when the computer-generated notice is inadequate, such as when two or more conditions or circumstances cause the change in payment or termination. 10200 Determining Initial and Continuing Eligibility for Blind or Otherwise Disabled Children and Adults in Foster Care The Medicaid waiver service case manager and the eligibility unit in the county department will continue to share responsibility for establishing initial and continuing eligibility for supplementation for blind or otherwise disabled children and adults in foster care. To assure coordination between the related activities of each unit, the procedures in the sections below are to be followed. 10205 Application Procedures for Children and Adults in Foster Care The Medicaid waiver service case manager will apply for supplementation for a child in foster care or will ensure that the adult in foster care or other “proper applicant” completes an application for supplementation, after it has been determined that the individual receives SSI. A. Application The Medicaid waiver service case manager will initiate the application of a foster child by completing the 693. (Refer to Appendix II for the form and special instruction for its completion for a child or adult in foster care.) The completed forms are to be forwarded immediately to the eligibility supervisor with a cover Form 799 stating that the individual receives SSI. B. Care Provider The care provider must meet requirements outlined in Section 12010-C. Form 797 and, as needed, 1801 must be completed and submitted to the eligibility worker. 28

body: 10210 Disposition of Application for Children and Adults in Foster Care The eligibility worker will determine eligibility for persons in foster care as for other persons for whom application for supplementation is made. For children in foster care, the notice of approval or denial will be sent to the foster care provider, who is to be shown as payee other than applicant on ZC87, at the SUP recipient’s address. For adults in foster care, the notice will be sent to the applicant/recipient. 10215 Continuing Eligibility Procedures for Children and Adults in Foster Care The eligibility worker will determine continued eligibility as indicated when changes are reported or information in the record appears inconsistent. Any notice of action as a result of a change will be sent as in the paragraph above. 10300 Legal Representative or Legal Guardian A person may be so physically or mentally handicapped that s/he cannot endorse his check by mark or signature. If so, s/he must have either a legal representative or a legal guardian if s/he is to receive assistance. Do not delay action on an application or hold a check pending appointment of a legal guardian or representative, if there is someone to act responsibly for a client who cannot act for him/herself. Endorsement by a person acting responsibly for a client after a client has touched the pen is acceptable. The person acting responsible for him cannot sign the eligibility or other forms for him unless appointed legal representative or legal guardian. It is, therefore, necessary that these provisions for establishing initial and continuing eligibility be temporary and referral to Adult Protective Services be made regarding his potential need for protection. A mentally handicapped person may require a legal guardian rather than a legal representative. The attorney’s recommendation should be followed. In some instances, even though a person is able to sign his check by signature or mark, s/he may want and/or need a legal representative or a legal guardian to protect his best interest. NOTE: An individual must be a legal representative or legal guardian to serve as an alternate payee for a SUP client (see FACETS Certification User Manual Section 3305). 29

Related procedures for delivery of Medicaid cards for non-SSI recipients are discussed in Section 24800. Referrals to adult services for neglect and/or abuse may be appropriate. body: [1] Chapter 11 Supplementation Eligibility Requirements Other Than Need 11000 General There are several eligibility conditions that must be met in addition to need (and receipt of SSI) to qualify for assistance under the supplementation programs. Requirements related to residence, citizenship and alienage are the same for all categories. In addition, a person must be considered permanently and totally disabled for APTD and for AB a person must be blind and must not publicly solicit aims. 11100 Age Accept SSA information on age and date of birth unless there exists substantial evidence that the SSA information is incorrect. Such discrepancies should be called to the attention of SSA and reconciled. Age requirements in supplementation are set out by category below. A. OAP – At least 65. OAP may be paid to a person in the month he becomes 65. B. APTD and AB–Under 65. When a person reaches 65 and continues eligible, terminate assistance and award under OAP, effective with the month after the birth month. Hold the notice on ZC58 when processing the termination and the award. 11200 Residence A person must be a resident of Alabama in order to receive supplementation and must by physically present in the state when his application is approved unless detained because of illness while temporarily outside of the state and residence requirements are otherwise met. The reason a person comes to Alabama to reside had no bearing on his residence for public assistance purposes, except in determining whether or not he is in the state for a temporary purpose. 2

body: 11204 Where to Apply A person applies for and receives aid in the county where he lives. (See Section 1135­ A) 11210 Who Has Residence A. A person over 21 is considered a resident of Alabama under one of the following conditions: 1. He is living in Alabama voluntarily, not for a temporary purpose, with the intent to make the state his home; or 2. He has entered Alabama either with a job commitment or to seek employment (whether or not currently employed) and is not receiving assistance from another state at the time his benefits from Alabama begin. B. A person over 21, incapable of stating intent to make Alabama his home, is considered a resident if he is living in Alabama and is not receiving assistance from another state at the time his benefits from Alabama begin. A person is considered to be incapable of stating intent if: 1. His IQ is 49 or less, or he has a mental age of 7 or less, based on tests acceptable to the state Department of Public Health; 2. He is judged legally incompetent; or 3. Medical or other documentation supports a finding that he is incapable of indicating intent. C. A person under 21 is considered to be a resident of Alabama for public assistance purposes if he is making his home in Alabama other than on a temporary basis. D. An applicant must be physically present in Alabama when his application is approved, unless detained because of illness while temporarily outside of the state and residence requirements are otherwise met. 3

body: 11215 Temporary Visits and Retention of Residence A. Temporary absence from the state does not affect residence unless it is for the purpose of establishing residence elsewhere. When there was no opportunity to talk with the person before he left Alabama or his absence is not known until he has already left, begin immediately to determine whether or not he retains Alabama residence. Send the person a letter by certified mail, return receipt requested, saying that the county department will presume that he has abandoned Alabama residence id he does not reply within 20 days. 1. Applicant – If the applicant does not respond within 20 days, deny the application. 2. Recipient – If the recipient does not respond within 20 days and it is evident he received the letter by the returned receipt, process a termination due to loss of residence. If he presents acceptable evidence to the contrary before expiration of the advance notice period, reinstate aid immediately. B. If the applicant or recipient is visiting only and, therefore, keeps his Alabama residence, continue or reaward aid if he is otherwise eligible and 1. Reach a mutual agreement as to the time he plans to return to Alabama. Enter the appropriate alert code and the month after the month of expected return as the alert date. (Refer to FACETS User Manual Sections 11999 & 13215.) Unless he is detained because of an emergency or other unusual circumstances, terminate assistance according to alert procedures. If failure to return is due to illness of the recipient or member of his family, he must substantiate this fact by furnishing the agency a written statement from a physician. 2. After his first three months of absence from Alabama, have the recipient report quarterly his plans and intent to return to Alabama. Advise him that aid will be discontinued at any time he fails to do so. NOTE: When a recipient plans to visit outside of this state for more than three months, send the 1389 to the public welfare agency in the other state, in order to obtain information or documentation of the applicant/recipient status in the other state. 4

body: 11225 Residence in Public and Private Institutions A. Public and Private Institutions Optional Supplementation payments cannot be made to a person who is a permanent resident of a nursing home. Payments may be made for up to three months to a recipient in a nursing home if a physician certifies that the person’s stay is likely not to exceed three months and the person needs to maintain former living arrangements. (See 1012-I.5) A person is not eligible for aid while he is a resident in a public institution. A person may receive aid, however, if he is living in a hospital or other types of private institutions. If in other types of private institutions, the following must apply: 1. The institution provides care for four or more unrelated persons and is licensed by the state Health Department as a personal care home; 2. The person is free to make his own arrangements, establish the terms of financial agreement with the institution, and determine the duration of his stay; 3. The person has not paid or transferred to the institution money or other valuables in sufficient amount to cover the cost of maintenance and care; and 4. The institution is not for mental disease. B. Educational and Vocational Institutions A person may receive aid if he is attending a public educational or vocational training institution, such as the State School for the Blind or a vocational training center. Questions as to eligibility in institutions should be cleared through the Family Assistance Division. The fact that an applicant/recipient is in a private facility which is not a medical treatment facility, and the duration of the stay, are immaterial to his eligibility for supplementation. A person in conditional release from an institution in which aid cannot be given (for instance, on “parole”, “trial visits”, etc.) if otherwise eligible, can receive aid, provided he is free of controls by the institution other than professional help or guidance. 5

body: Policies governing accrual rights as set out in Section 10130 apply for persons entering and leaving institutions in which aid cannot be given. 11300 Citizenship and Alienage – General To be eligible for SSI and, therefore, supplementation, a person must be a citizen of the United States or, if an alien must have been lawfully admitted to this country for permanent residence. Accept receipt of SSI benefits as meeting this requirement. “Grandfathered in” non-SSI SUP recipients have previously been determined to otherwise meet this requirement. 11500 Permanent and Total Disability A person must meet the Federal requirements of being permanently and totally disabled to the extent that he is substantially prevented from engaging in any useful occupation within his competence. The determination of eligibility for SSI (or RSDI) benefits by the SSA, based on disability is sufficient for establishing disability in APTD. If a “grandfathered in” non-SSI SUP recipient does not receive RSDI based on disability, any re-evaluation of his continued eligibility based on disability will be made by the Alabama Medicaid Agency. See procedures in Section 11510 below. 11505 Definitions (A) (B) (C) Given below are definitions of: permanent disability; total disability; and physical or mental impairment(s). A. Permanent Disability A permanent disability is a serious anatomical, physiological, or psychological impairment which can be expected to result in death, or which has lasted or can be expected to last for a continuous period of not less than 12 months. This does not include conditions known to be temporary or transient. B. Total Disability The determination of total disability requires two separate judgments: (a) an assessment of an individual(s) remaining capacity to perform physical or mental activities; (b) an assessment of an individual’s physical or mental capacity to 6
citation_label: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2 91
heading: Page 91

body: perform a job or group of jobs falling within the individual’s vocational spectrum, if the capacity to perform is less than the demands of the job, then the individual is disabled. C. Physical or Mental Impairment A physical or mental impairment is any serious anatomical, physiological or psychological abnormality which can be medically demonstrated. Such abnormalities must manifest themselves as signs or laboratory findings apart from symptoms which are an individual(s) own perception of his physical or mental impairment(s). 11510 Procedures for Requesting Decision on Re-Examination for Continuing Eligibility of a Non-RSDI Non-SSI SUP Recipient It is the eligibility worker’s responsibility to see that re-evaluations are made and submitted to the state Department of Human Resources, Attention: Family Assistance Division, when they are necessary to establish continuing eligibility. The Family Assistance Division determines whether the individual is or is not disabled. If the individual is not determined disabled, steps to terminate must be taken. Reexaminations must be made when recommended by the Family Assistance Division; when the client has had surgery or treatment; or when the worker notes an improvement in a condition previously diagnosed as a permanent impairment. Follow FACETS alert procedures to assure that re-examinations are promptly processed. A. Physical Changes 1. Medical Data To determine a person’s continued disability for the APTD category, there must be an examination and diagnosis by a legally licensed doctor of medicine or a legally licensed osteopathic physician. a. Medical information is frequently available from such sources as mental hospitals, mental health clinics, county health departments, cancer control clinics, VRS, CCS and VA. Do not authorize use of the 667 until clearance has been made with these agencies to which the person may be known. b. Use the 667 to obtain medical data from a physician when not available otherwise. If a public facility for the examination is not available, the recipient may use a doctor or physician of his choice and the cost up to $15 7

body: can be paid as an administrative expense. If the recipient is dissatisfied with the decision of the review team and wishes a second examination, he may do so at his own expense. 2. Social Data The worker must complete a 668 which provides detailed social information. This additional social information is not to be attached to the 667 before it is sent to the examining physician. NOTE: Re-examinations are not necessary when the physician has stated in writing that the person’s condition cannot improve. In such cases, resubmit the original examination to the Alabama Medicaid Agency and additional medical information will be requested if needed. B. Social Changes It is also the worker’s responsibility to submit new social information to the Family Assistance Division, when there are changes which may have a direct bearing on a recipient’s continued eligibility for APTD. For example, the worker may find that a recipient has new or increased abilities due to changes in environment, training, education, or adjustment to his impairment. If so, the worker submits a new 668, giving his observations and other current social information. The Family Assistance Division will make the decision as to whether the person still meets the criteria of total disability. In some instances, a new physical examination may be recommended. NOTE: When the county department submits current medical and/or social information for review, the worker should always attach it to the form(s) on which the Family Assistance Division based previous decisions, as well as forms notifying the county department of the decision of the Family Assistance Division. 11600 Blindness To receive AB, a person must be considered blind according to Federal requirements. The determination of eligibility for SSI (or RSDI) benefits by the Social Security Administration based on blindness is sufficient for establishing blindness in AB. 8
citation_label: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2 93

metadata.title: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2

body: If a “grandfathered in” non-SSI SUP recipient does not receive RSDI based on blindness, the Family Assistance Division will determine whether he can continue to be considered blind under AB if a re-evaluation is made. 11605 Definition of Blindness A person is considered blind who has no vision, or whose vision with correcting glasses is so defective as to prevent the performance of ordinary activities for which eyesight is essential. Central visual acuity of 20/200 or less in better eye with correcting glasses is considered as economic blindness. A person, however, may be considered blind if his central visual acuity is more than 20/200 in the better eye with correcting glasses but a rough test shows him to have a marked field defect. The marked field defect is one in which the peripheral field has contracted to such an extent that the widest diameter of the visual field subtends an angular distance no greater than 20 degrees. 11610 Procedures for Requesting Decision on Re-Examination for Continuing Eligibility of Non-RSDI, Non-SSI SUP Recipient For assistance purposes, blindness may be said to continue until re-examination establishes that the recipient has vision in excess of that in the state’s definition. Re­ examinations are necessary to establish continued eligibility only under the following conditions: 1. Re-examination has been recommended by the eye examiner or on the report form. In such case, follow FACETS alert procedures, if recommended at a future time, or follow procedures in Section 10125-G, if recommended currently. 2. The person has had treatment or surgery 3. The worker notes an obvious improvement in the person’s vision. Re-examination should be submitted to the Family Assistance Division. Both the original and duplicate of PA-701 or PSD-87 must be sent. If additional information is needed, it will be requested from the county office. The Department of Human Resources can pay $15 for the cost of the re-examination as an administrative expense, provided the individual cannot secure such re-examination without cost from a free public or private diagnostic facility. 9

body: 11700 Furnishing Social Security Numbers To be eligible for SSI and, therefore, Supplementation, a person must apply for or furnish his social security number. Accept receipt of SSI benefits as meeting this requirement. “Grandfathered in” non-SSI SUP recipients have previously been determined to otherwise meet this requirement by furnishing a number or meeting agency enumeration requirements. body: Chapter 12 [1] Supplementation Need 12000 General Need is the basic eligibility requirement for aid. It represents the amount of money needed from the agency to supplement a person’s income/resources to meet the state department’s defined supplement to SSI. To carry out requirements and intent of state and federal laws, the state department has defined within this chapter policies, procedures, and certain standards for evaluating income/resources and the amount of supplements. Policies and procedures for determining need and payment are mandatory upon the state and county departments to assure uniform administration and equitable treatment. 12005 Need and Payment Standards The standards in the Supplementation Program vary dependent on the type of supplement, living arrangement and level of care. Information concerning the amounts of these standards is found in tables in Appendix I. 12010 Supplements A. Independent Homelife Care Supplement (applicable only to persons receiving a Personal Care Supplement as of September 30, 1986.) Some recipients require independent homelife care in order to be able to live as self-sufficiently as possible outside of the nursing home. When the individual’s needs are not totally met by home health care programs such as VRS’s Homebound Program, VA’s Aid and Attendant Care, and Alabama Medicaid Agency’s Home Care Programs and the department’s Homemaker Services Program and s/he is required to pay for care, or additional care s/he may be eligible for a supplement while residing in the home, hospital, personal care home, or a foster care home. 1. Types of Service Covered Services considered as enabling a person to live as self-sufficiently as possible include help (1) (2) with any one or more of the following: conducting a prescribed exercise routine: changing bandages or dressings on the advice of the physician; (3) using prostheses or ambulation 3
citation_label: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2 98
heading: Page 98

metadata.title: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2

body: (4) (5) (6) aids; locomotion; maintaining an acceptable state of cleanliness; maintaining adequate nutritional standards in the purchase and preparation of food; (7) maintaining orientation to (8) (9) time, place and events; reminding of the need for medication; performing other activities needed to help a person care for himself when his illness or disability prevents him from adequately doing so. Companionship for pleasure or convenience rather than protection and necessity is not included in this definition nor is the provision of chore services such as transportation, home or yard maintenance, running errands, etc. Individuals needing only companionship or chore services are not eligible. 2. Levels of Independence Persons requiring services described above have varying levels of independence as discussed below. a. The individual is confined to a chair or bed; or The individual has mental limitations which alone or together with physical limitations cause him to require regular care from someone else throughout the day or night. (Level A.) NOTE: While there are individuals confined to wheelchairs who appear to be self-sufficient and not to require regular independent homelife care, the fact a person is confined to a chair is considered, for agency purposes, sufficient evidence to warrant classification in Level A. b. The individual is ambulatory, but requires regular care (not necessarily throughout the day or night) from someone else. (Level B) 3. Eligibility Determination Secure information and/or verification as indicated below, to establish that the person continues to require independent homelife care and that s/he has a care provider as defined in 12010-C below whom he is paying for the independent homelife care. a. Obtain new medical information from a legally licensed physician of the client’s choice, using the 696, if the need for care was not described as a permanent as indicated on the previous 696. Also, secure a new 696 when improvement is noted in a condition previously described as permanent and when a person’s level of independence appears to have changed enough to warrant a different classification. 4
citation_label: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2 99

body: b. Obtain social information from the recipient’s own statements, as well as observations of the worker and other persons in a position to know each time a new medical is completed. Social information refers to how the person generally spends his time, to what extent and from whom he usually receives help in daily activities, etc. This information may be recorded in the narrative or on Form 668. c. Verify the care arrangement every twelve months by setting a C600 alert. The C600 alert notice is sent to the client around the 20th of the month prior to the alert month. The alert must be completed and signed by the client and care provider and returned to the county th th office no later than the 15 of the alert month. If the alert is not cleared by the 15 of the alert month, the SUP case must be terminated effective the end of the alert month. If the alert is received and reflects a change, contact the client immediately to process a new 797. NOTE: When the care provider is employed by an agency to provide care to the client, the agency’s name, rather than the individual’s, is to be shown on the 797. d. For recipients for whom a new 696 is completed, the decision on continued eligibility based on need for care will be made by the eligibility worker using the physician’s recommendation and social information. When the worker does not concur with the physician’s recommendation, the decision on eligibility must be made by the state department. In such cases, mail a photocopy of the completed 696 and social information to your PA Field Supervisor requesting an eligibility decision. The field supervisor will advise the county department of the decision, which is binding until additional information is received indicating the need for reconsideration. e. Terminate the supplementation payment when the individual is no longer eligible under the criteria in effect prior to October 1, 1986 (unless he is now eligible under criteria in Section B below) or when there is a break in his eligibility for any reason. 4. Payment Levels The SUP payment levels for care are found in Appendix I, Table of Special Needs of Applicants and Recipients. The payment level for personal care in a private home or personal care facility is dependent upon the individual’s level of independence. There is only one payment level for personal care in a foster home. a. SSI Recipients 5

body: Persons receiving SSI are entitled to the full supplement for which they are determined eligible. A separate income/resource eligibility determination is not necessary to establish financial eligibility. A person who “receives” SSI, as used here, includes persons who have been determined eligible for SSI but whose SSI payment is in suspense (or withheld) for a reason other than ineligibility. b. Non-SSI Recipients (See Section 12015.) Persons not receiving SSI are entitled to a supplement in the amount of the difference between the full supplement and the amount by which their countable income exceeds the basic need standard, provided that the rounded amount of the supplement is as much as a dollar. NOTE: A person may be income-eligible for payment level “A” but not for “B” and may be functionally eligible for only “B”. In such case, the appropriate termination code is one for which the notice indicates that termination is due to income. When a recipient is income- eligible for both payment levels and his functional eligibility changes from “A” to “B” or vice versa, make a change to the level of care on ZC26 and notify the client of the change using Form 657. B. Specialized independent Homelife Care supplement (applicable to persons applying for supplementation on or after October 1, 1986) To be eligible for this supplement an individual must be receiving SSI and Medicaid waiver services at the skilled nursing facility (SNF) criteria and homelife care services as defined in A.1 above in a private home or foster home from someone whom they pay to provide such care. NOTE: “Specialized” refers to the circumstances of the individual in relation to the point in time when s/he applied for state SUP not the type of services. 1. Eligibility Process Responsibilities for specialized independent homelife care supplementation cases will be shared by staff at the department of Senior Services or the department of Public Health and eligibility staff. The eligibility worker receives and processes the application packet which should include a completed and signed 693 and 797 and, as needed, 1801. The cover Form 799 will state that the individual receives SSI and may indicate the individual has been approved for Medicaid waiver services at the skilled nursing facility (SNF) level. (NOTE: If no level is designated, obtain the determination of SNF level from the state office by mailing legible copies of the waiver services packet to the Family Assistance Division.) a. Verify the status of those persons receiving Medicaid waiver services by the use of the MSIQ screen. Waiver recipients are shown in field LTC with provider code 008500520. 6
citation_label: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2 101
heading: Page 101

metadata.title: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2

body: b. Award supplementation payments according to usual procedures in the FACETS User Manual. Accrual rights are to the date of application which is the day the properly completed application packet is received by the eligibility unit/worker. c. Verify the provider of care every twelve months following procedures in A.3.c above. d. Process changes and terminations, as appropriate, based on information received from the individual’s Medicaid waiver case manager. If information is received from another source, including the client or care provider, confirm it with the case manager before taking action. If the client fails to respond to the C600 alert notice, contact the case manager for current information and then take appropriate action. Process terminations using regular closure codes. Hold the automated notice and provide notice using 657 as appropriate/needed. e. Verify SSI status in the alert month. 2. Payment Levels The payment levels for specialized independent homelife care in a private home or foster home are found in Appendix I. Persons eligible for specialized independent homelife care receive the full supplement for that particular living arrangement. C. Homelife Care Provider Requirements 1. General The care provider of persons receiving supplements as described in A and B above must be an employee of a certified home health agency; or (if the county health department does not make homelife services available in the county or certain cases) an individual who declares in writing that s/he has at least a 6th grade education; is physically and mentally able to provide care as evidenced by a medical statement (Form 1801); has not been convicted of a serious crime; and has not been fired or forced t resign from a position as a care provider due to dereliction of duty. The care provider cannot be of any of the following degrees of relationship to the applicant/recipient; child, stepchild, adoptive child, son or daughter-in-law; parent, stepparent, adoptive parent; spouse; or brother, sister, brother-in-law, sister-in-law. A certified home health agency is an agency so certified by the Alabama Department of Public Health, Division of Licensure and Certification. 7

body: 2. Documentation of the non-availability of Certified Home Health Services If the County Health Department does not provide services in the county, secure a written statement from the health department to this effect and maintain it in department official files. Make a narrative entry in individual case records to indicate services are not available through the health department. If the County Health Department provides services but cannot provide them in certain cases, have the client secure a written statement from the Health Department indicating services are not available specifically to him/her and maintain it in the case record. D. Paying for Care Requirement 1. Definition A person is considered to be paying for care if payment for a month’s care is made timely. Timely is defined as follows: agency provider-within the billing cycle of the provider agency; or individual provider-no later than the month following the month in which care is provided unless the individual provider requires it earlier. 2. Example (Agency and Individual) Services (care) provided in June, billed for in July – due in July. To be considered paying for care for June, payment to the agency or individual must be made in July. If payment is not made until August or later, there has been a break in eligibility and the individual is no longer eligible. 12011 Care in Cerebral Palsy Treatment Center – APTD Eligible recipients receiving care in Cerebral Palsy Treatment Centers who were eligible for optional supplementation as of June 1, 1981, may continue to receive a monthly payment to supplement their other income to help pay for the cost of their care and certain basic needs. These APTD recipients are not entitled to Medicaid coverage by virtue or receipt of an APTD money payment. Those who receive SSI have Medicaid coverage under that program. A. Limitation of Coverage APTD for persons in Cerebral Palsy Treatment Centers is available only to a person who was in a Cerebral Palsy Treatment Center and eligible for APTD June 1981. “Eligible for” includes persons actually receiving APTD in the center in June 1981, as well as persons who before June 8

metadata.title: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2

body: 1981: were 18 years of age or older and in a Cerebral Palsy Treatment Center, and had applied for APTD before June 1981 and were later determined eligible. Eligibility continues only for as long as he remains in the Center and receives SSI, or would receive SSI except for income. (An interruption for any length of time in the stay in the center has no effect on the person’s eligibility upon his return to the center.) B. Amount of the Supplement The SUP payment level for persons in a Cerebral Palsy Treatment Center is $196 per month. No separate statement of income/resources nor budget is necessary for persons who receive SSI; their payment is the full supplement. Persons who do not receive SSI due to excessive income are eligible for a supplement in the amount of the difference between $196 and the amount of income in excess of the SSI Federal Benefit Rate (FBR). 12012 Mandatory Money Payment Recipient in a Nursing Home Money payments must be made to a person who received a money payment from the Department of Human Resources in December 1973, and who is presently in a nursing home and has income less than the personal needs allowance in effect in December 1973 ($48-OAP, $22-APTD, and $32-AB). A contact with the recipient must be made annually to verify presence in the nursing home and that there have been no other changes. Otherwise, determine continued eligibility as needed on these cases according to usual procedures. A. SSI Recipients SUP payment levels are $23 in OAP or $7 in AB. (There is no mandatory money payment in APTD for SSI recipients in nursing homes.) B. Non-SSI Recipients SUP payments must be computed according to policies and procedures in effect in 1973. Therefore, as needed, mail the case record to the Family Assistance Division for a determination of initial or continuing eligibility. When the case record is returned with that division’s decision, process the award, change or termination on FACETS according to instructions. 12015 “Grandfathered-In” Non-SSI Optional SUP Recipients 9

body: Eligible Non-SSI SUP recipients who were receiving SUP (as non-SSI recipients) as of March 7, 1986 may continue to receive a monthly payment (and Medicaid) to pay for personal care as long as they remain otherwise eligible including the requirement of being ineligible for SSI solely due to income. Policies and procedures related to income and resources in Chapter 12 are applicable only to these grandfathered non-SSI recipients. The policies and procedures related to need for personal care, establishing continuing eligibility, handling changes, etc., remain applicable to these grandfathered cases just as they are applicable to SSI SUP recipients. Grandfathered status under this provision is lost if the non-SSI recipient becomes ineligible for SUP for any reason or begins to receive SSI. 12100 Grant Computation - General The purpose of the grant computation is to substantiate: A. Monthly payments to or in behalf of an eligible person: or B. Denial of aid based on lack of need. A grant computation is not necessary when a person in ineligible for some reason other than need. Grant computations to determine eligibility and amount of payment are made by computer based on the level of care entered on ZC26 and the income (if any) entered on ZC24, ZC29 and/or ZC30. If an applicant/recipient is ineligible due to income, error message “Net Income Exceeds Standard” is received on ZC45 and ZC58. In such cases, print either screen to place in the case record to document ineligibility due to income. Process a denial or termination on ZC58. 12105 Grant Computation for SSI Recipients When FACETS is coded to indicate receipt of SSI, (SSI income entered on ZC30) the amount of payment will be determined by computer according to the level of care code entered on ZC26. 12110 Grant Computation for Non-SSI Recipients with No Spouse When FACETS is coded to indicate no SSI (no SSI income listed on ZC30) and no spouse, the computer will calculate the amount of payment using income entered according to the steps below. (Any manual payment computations must follow the same steps.) Deemed income from a parent(s) must be computed manually (Contact the FAD for instructions. See Section 12125-C) and entered as cash contributions on ZC30. A. Unearned income, excluding income based on need will be totaled. 10
citation_label: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2 105
heading: Page 105

metadata.title: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2

body: B. The general exclusion ($20) will be subtracted. C. Earned income will be totaled. D. Any remainder of the $20 general exclusion will be subtracted from total earnings. E. $65 and ½ of the remainder will be subtracted from total earnings after any general exclusion. F. Unearned income remaining after Step “B”, earned income remaining after Step “E”, and income based on need, if any, will be totaled. G. The appropriate FBR (Federal Benefit Rate) will then be subtracted from total countable income to get excess income. If excess income is a figure ending in .50, it will be rounded up at this point. Otherwise, excess income will remain unrounded in this step. H. Excess income will be subtracted from the SUP payment level for the level of care code shown. The remainder, rounded to the nearest dollar, is the grant amount. 12115 Grant Computation for Non-SSI Recipients with Ineligible Spouse When FACETS is coded to indicate the recipient has an ineligible spouse and no SSI (no SSI income listed on ZC30), the computer will calculate the amount of payment, using income entered, according to the same steps as in 12110. (Any manual payment computations made by the worker must follow steps in 12110.) Deemed income as computed by the worker according to Section 12125-B must be entered as cash contributions on ZC30. 12120 Grant Computation for Non-SSI Couple SUP Recipients When the ZC26 for a SUP recipient is coded to indicate s/he has a spouse eligible for SUP (spouse added to unit) and no SSI (no SSI listed on ZC30), the computer will calculate the amount of payment, using income entered. (Any manual payment computation made by the worker must follow these steps.) Income amounts to be entered on each spouse’s ZC30 must be the total for both spouses. Screens must be coded to indicate that the couple FBR, either full or reduced, is to be used in computations. A. The computer calculation will follow steps “A” through “F” in Section 12110 to determine total couple countable income for each spouse. B. The appropriate FBR will then be subtracted to get couple excess income. 11
citation_label: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2 106
heading: Page 106

metadata.title: Alabama DHR Public Assistance Payment Manual: Appendix N, Section 2

body: C. Couple excess income will be subtracted from the SUP payment level times 2. The remainder will then be divided by 2 to determine the grant amount, rounded to whole dollars, if necessary, according to either of the following: 1. If couple excess income obtained in step “B” was an even dollar amount with or without cents, or an odd dollar amount with cents, the grant amount for each spouse will be rounded to the nearest dollar: 49 cents or less, down; 50 cents or more, up. 2. If couple excess income obtained in step “B” was an odd dollar amount with no cents, the wife’s grant amount will be rounded up to the next dollar and the husband’s will be rounded down. 12121 Maximum and Minimum Payments The maximum monthly payment is the full supplement for which a person is eligible as determined by the case category and level of care. The minimum payment is $1. 12125 Deeming of Income Deemed income is income attributed to another whether or not the income is actually available to that person. Husband and wife, parent(s) and child(ren) living together generally share income and responsibility to each other and they generally share income and resources. Income is deemed only from spouse to spouse, or from parent and stepparent to child under 18. Consider all income available to an individual or couple in the deeming process, unless it is defined as income not to be budgeted, Section 12230, or is listed in A below. A. Items not included in Deeming 1. Assistance based on need (12210-G) or income based on need (12210-H), which is a. Furnished by the federal, state or local government; or b. SSI payments; and c. Income used to determine eligibility or payment of assistance or income based on need. 2. Monies considered not currently available. 12
